question: The foundational people (HR) metrics that every manager should track include: Turnover Rate, Time-to-Fill, and Employee Engagement Score. How are these calculated and what do they reveal?
options: Only compensation levels matter for assessing workforce health

Each metric reveals a different dimension of workforce health: Turnover Rate = (Number of separations in a period / Average headcount) × 100%; a 20% annual turnover rate means 1 in 5 employees leaves per year; separately tracking voluntary turnover (employee chose to leave) vs. involuntary turnover (company-initiated) reveals whether turnover reflects employee dissatisfaction vs. performance management. Time-to-Fill = Days from job requisition approved to offer accepted; measures recruiting process speed and talent market competitiveness; a 90-day time-to-fill for a software engineer role may indicate: insufficient talent pipeline, slow hiring process, or non-competitive compensation offer. Employee Engagement Score: percentage of employees 'actively engaged' (Gallup defines as 'enthusiastic about and committed to work and workplace'); organizations with top-quartile engagement show 21% higher profitability; the three measurements together create a workforce health dashboard: high turnover + long time-to-fill + low engagement = talent crisis requiring urgent intervention; low turnover + fast time-to-fill + high engagement = healthy talent system
Employee headcount is the only HR metric that matters for business performance
HR metrics are only relevant for HR professionals, not operational managers
explanation: People analytics as a business tool: the Gallup Q12 engagement survey (12 questions that measure engagement dimensions from 'I know what's expected of me at work' to 'I have a best friend at work') is the most widely-used engagement diagnostic. Gallup's meta-analysis of 112,000 business units found that business units with top-quartile engagement show: 21% higher profitability, 17% higher productivity, 10% higher customer satisfaction, 41% lower absenteeism, 59% less turnover. These correlations make engagement a business performance predictor, not just an HR vanity metric. The turnover cost calculation: SHRM estimates replacing an employee costs 50-200% of annual salary. A company with 1,000 employees at $70K average salary and 25% turnover replaces 250 employees/year; at 100% replacement cost = $17.5M annually. Reducing turnover from 25% to 15% saves $7M/year — a compelling business case for engagement investment.

question: Google's People Analytics team conducted 'Project Oxygen' (2009) and 'Project Aristotle' (2012). Project Oxygen found that manager quality matters enormously; Project Aristotle found that team psychological safety is the strongest predictor of team performance. How should an HR team use these findings to improve organizational performance?
options: Research findings from Google are only applicable to other technology companies

Research-based insights require organizational infrastructure to translate into performance improvement: using Project Oxygen: the finding that manager quality predicts team performance implies: (1) Selection: the promotion criteria for managers must include management skills (not just individual contribution excellence); (2) Assessment: managers must be assessed regularly on management behaviors (upward feedback from their team); Google's 'Googlegeist' survey includes manager-specific questions; (3) Development: once specific management behaviors are identified (Oxygen's 8 behaviors), development programs, coaching, and resources can be targeted at improving each behavior; (4) Accountability: managers who score consistently low on team feedback must have performance improvement plans. Using Project Aristotle: psychological safety means team members feel safe taking risks and being vulnerable — they can raise concerns, admit mistakes, and share ideas without fear of punishment or ridicule; the implication: (1) Norms: team leaders must explicitly establish norms for how disagreement and failure are handled; (2) Leading examples: managers who admit their own mistakes and uncertainty model psychological safety; (3) Meeting design: meeting structures where all voices are heard (not just the most senior or most vocal) create safety; (4) Promotion criteria: if people are promoted for having the 'right answers' rather than raising the right questions, the incentive system undermines psychological safety
Manager quality and team psychological safety are HR concerns with no effect on business outcomes
Psychological safety improvements always reduce performance standards and accountability
explanation: Psychological safety measurement: Amy Edmondson (Harvard Business School) developed the psychological safety concept and the 7-item survey scale used to measure it. The scale items include: 'If you make a mistake on this team, it is often held against you' (reverse-scored), 'Members of this team are able to bring up problems and tough issues,' and 'It is difficult to ask other members of this team for help' (reverse-scored). Teams in the bottom quartile on psychological safety show higher quality issues (problems not raised early), lower innovation (people don't propose new ideas), and higher attrition (talented people who don't feel safe leave). The Project Aristotle finding: Google analyzed 180 teams over 2 years; psychological safety was the #1 predictor of team performance, above individual talent levels, team composition, and even management quality. This confirmed Edmondson's academic research in a real-world, large-scale business context. The safety-accountability balance: Amy Edmondson distinguishes between 'comfort zone' (low safety, low standards — teams that don't feel safe and aren't challenged) and 'learning zone' (high safety, high standards — teams that feel safe and are ambitious). High psychological safety enables high performance standards, not lower ones, because people can raise problems before they become failures.

question: A technology company tracks 'regrettable turnover' separately from 'total turnover.' In Q3, total voluntary turnover was 15%, but 'regrettable turnover' (high-performing employees the company wishes had stayed) was 22% of the voluntary departures — meaning 3.3% of the total workforce were high performers who left. The CHRO says this is the most important metric to track. Why is regrettable turnover more informative than total turnover?
options: Total turnover is always a more meaningful metric than regrettable turnover

Regrettable turnover measures quality of departures rather than volume; losing top performers has asymmetric impact: the 15% total voluntary turnover might reflect a healthy mix: some high performers leaving (regrettable), some average performers leaving to pursue new opportunities (neutral), some low performers choosing to leave rather than be managed out (positive, as they may have been below the company's standards). The regrettable turnover insight: 3.3% of total workforce being high performers who left means: the company's best contributors are leaving at a disproportionate rate (if high performers are 20% of workforce, but 22% of departures are high performers, they're 10% more likely to leave than average employees); this is a warning signal: top talent is being pushed or pulled away at above-average rates; the financial impact asymmetry: a high performer generates 2-5× the output of an average performer (research suggests this in knowledge work); losing 3.3% of the workforce in high performers is not equivalent to losing 3.3% of average workers — the organizational impact is disproportionate; the diagnostic: why are high performers leaving at elevated rates? Compensation (market is paying more), development (limited growth opportunities), management (a specific manager driving departures), or culture (misalignment between stated values and lived reality)
Exit surveys are always reliable indicators of why employees leave
All voluntary turnover represents equivalent organizational loss
explanation: Regrettable vs. non-regrettable turnover: this distinction requires managers to evaluate each departure — a judgment call that creates accountability for talent decisions. When a manager must classify a departure as 'regrettable,' it forces reflection: could we have prevented this? Did we have warning signs? Is there a systemic problem driving these departures? The Netflix 'Keeper Test': Netflix's culture deck (widely shared) described the 'Keeper Test' — managers should regularly ask themselves 'if this person told me they were leaving, would I fight to keep them?' If the answer is no, Netflix argues the manager should proactively manage that person out to create space for someone they'd fight to keep. This reverses the turnover measurement: rather than just tracking who leaves, it asks managers to proactively assess who they want to keep. The exit interview reliability problem: employees often give socially acceptable reasons for leaving in exit interviews ('better opportunity', 'more money') rather than real reasons ('my manager was terrible', 'the culture is toxic'). Stay interviews (asking current employees what would cause them to leave) and anonymous surveys are more reliable than exit interviews for diagnosing retention risk.

question: A retail company has 50,000 hourly employees across 400 stores. The CHRO wants to implement a people analytics program to predict which employees are likely to resign in the next 30 days. The predictive model uses: days since last pay raise, manager's team satisfaction score, number of missed shifts in last 30 days, and geographic area unemployment rate. What ethical and practical considerations should govern this analytics program?
options: Predictive analytics in HR are always unethical and should be prohibited

Predictive models must be governed to prevent discrimination, protect privacy, and ensure transparent use: the analytical ethics framework: (1) Disparate impact risk: the model must be tested to ensure it doesn't predict resignation probability differently by race, gender, age, or disability status; if the model uses features that correlate with protected characteristics (e.g., geographic area unemployment rate may correlate with race/ethnicity), the model may have disparate impact even without explicitly using protected characteristics; (2) Data transparency: employees should know that behavioral data (missed shifts, pay history) is used in predictive models; the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCPA) implications: using predictive scores in employment decisions may require disclosure; (3) How the prediction is used: there's a meaningful difference between: 'prediction triggers a manager conversation to understand the employee's needs' (potentially positive intervention) vs. 'prediction triggers pre-emptive firing before the employee can resign' (potentially discriminatory and harmful); (4) Feedback loops: if the model predicts employees in a certain store are high-flight-risk based on the manager's low satisfaction score, the right intervention is improving the manager, not managing out the employees; (5) Model accuracy: false positives (predicting resignation for people who weren't going to leave) create harmful interventions for employees who weren't actually planning to leave
HR predictive analytics always improves employee outcomes without ethical considerations
Predictive analytics should only use publicly available demographic data
explanation: HR analytics ethics: IBM, Workday, and SAP have all developed people analytics platforms that include churn prediction. The ethical governance around these tools is active and evolving. The Illinois AI Video Interview Act (2020): Illinois was the first state to require notice to job candidates when AI/algorithms analyze video interviews; the broader trend toward AI transparency legislation (EU AI Act includes 'high-risk AI systems' that include employment-related AI) will increasingly regulate how predictive models are used in HR. The Amazon warehouse algorithms controversy: Amazon's algorithms tracked workers' 'time off task' and could automatically generate disciplinary documentation — criticized as dehumanizing and creating conditions where workers felt surveilled. The flight risk prediction ethical line: using prediction to understand why employees might leave (and address root causes — pay equity, management quality, career development) is beneficial; using prediction to pre-emptively discipline or terminate employees before they resign is harmful and likely illegal. The SHRM (Society for Human Resource Management) ethical guidelines for people analytics: recommend transparency with employees about data use, human review of algorithmic decisions, and regular bias audits of predictive models.

question: Costco's employee turnover is ~8%/year (vs. retail industry average of 65%). Costco pays significantly above industry average wages ($26+/hour average) and offers health insurance, 401k matching, and advancement opportunities. Costco's labor cost per unit is higher than Walmart's, but Costco's profitability per employee is significantly higher. What does this case reveal about the relationship between compensation, retention, and productivity?
options: High wages always reduce profitability because they increase labor cost without improving outcomes

Efficiency wage theory: paying above-market wages can be more profitable than paying market wages when productivity and retention effects offset the cost premium: the Costco math: (1) Costco labor cost: $26/hour × full benefits vs. Walmart $16/hour + fewer benefits; Costco labor costs are ~40% higher per hour; (2) The retention dividend: Costco's 8% annual turnover vs. 65% retail average; replacing an employee costs $3,000-5,000 (recruiting, training, productivity loss during ramp-up) at the hourly level; Walmart with 65% turnover replaces 65% of its workforce annually; at 100,000 employees × 65% × $3,500 = $227M/year in turnover costs; Costco with 8% turnover × 100,000 × $3,500 = $28M; turnover cost difference: $199M/year; (3) Productivity: Costco generates more revenue per employee than Walmart ($250K+ vs. $200K) because: experienced employees (low turnover = long average tenure) are more productive and knowledgeable; customer service quality is higher (happy employees → better service → more purchases); shrinkage (theft) is lower (employees invested in the company's success); (4) Net effect: the higher wages attract applicants who are more selective and committed; the efficiency wage hypothesis (Akerlof, Shapiro-Stiglitz) predicts this outcome: wages above market reduce shirking (employees don't want to lose a job they value), improve selection (better candidates apply for better-paying jobs), and increase retention
All companies should immediately raise wages to match Costco's model
The retail industry always requires high turnover as part of its business model
explanation: Efficiency wage theory: the economics of above-market wages was formalized by George Akerlof's efficiency wage model — workers who earn above-market wages have more to lose if they are fired (they can't easily find equivalent pay), so they work harder and more carefully. The Costco performance evidence: Forbes, HBS, and multiple academic papers have documented Costco's performance advantage. Revenue per square foot (~$1,100 vs. Walmart's ~$400) partly reflects Costco's higher-income warehouse club model, but also reflects productivity and shrinkage differences. The In-N-Out Burger model: In-N-Out starts employees at $18-20/hour (significantly above fast food average), offers health insurance for part-timers, and promotes almost exclusively internally; their turnover is dramatically lower than McDonald's and their customer service ratings significantly higher. The living wage vs. minimum wage debate: the economic argument for the living wage isn't charity — it's that paying wages insufficient to meet basic needs creates turnover, reduces productivity, and ultimately costs more in management overhead than the wage savings. The Walmart turnover disclosure: Walmart has historically not published its turnover rate; estimates from industry analysts suggest 65-70% annual hourly employee turnover, representing $1-2B in annual replacement costs.

question: A technology company is planning to lay off 15% of its workforce (1,500 people). The CEO wants to announce and execute the layoffs within 48 hours ('rip the band-aid'). The CHRO recommends a 4-week process with individual manager conversations, severance packages, career transition support, and a communications plan. What people metrics should inform whether the 48-hour approach will cost more than it saves?
options: Layoffs should always be executed as quickly as possible to minimize productivity disruption

The cost of poorly executed layoffs often exceeds the savings; people metrics illuminate the full cost: the 48-hour approach hidden costs: (1) Survivor turnover: employees who remain ('survivors') often experience guilt, anxiety, and loss of trust; poor layoff execution increases survivor voluntary turnover; if 10% of the 9,500 survivors quit within 6 months due to poor execution, that's 950 additional departures at $5,000 replacement cost = $4.75M (more than the severance savings for a hasty process); (2) Glassdoor impact: a poorly executed layoff generates immediate Glassdoor reviews (interviewing candidates research Glassdoor); companies with 2.5-star ratings post-layoff have increased time-to-fill by 40%+ and reduced offer acceptance rates; (3) Employer brand damage: if the layoff affects roles the company will need to hire for in 12-18 months, the 48-hour rip generates Glassdoor reviews that increase future hiring costs; (4) Legal risk: WARN Act (US) requires 60-day notice for layoffs of 100+ employees; violations: pay + benefits for the 60-day period plus attorney fees; (5) The CHRO's ROI case: 4-week process costs: manager time (moderate), severance upgrade (incremental), outplacement services ($500-2,000/person = $750K-3M); 4-week process benefits: survivor retention improvement (avoids 950 departures = $4.75M), Glassdoor score preservation (avoids $1-2M in increased recruiting cost), legal compliance
All layoffs require the same amount of time regardless of company size or complexity
The fastest layoff execution always results in the lowest total cost
explanation: Layoff execution cost research: McKinsey, Korn Ferry, and Challenger Gray & Christmas all have research documenting that poorly executed layoffs consistently result in higher total costs than carefully executed ones. The WARN Act compliance: the Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act requires 60 days' advance notice for layoffs affecting 100+ employees; tech companies that violate WARN Act (Twitter/X in 2022 faced WARN Act class action lawsuits) pay back pay and benefits for the 60-day period. Twitter's 2022 layoffs: Elon Musk's rapid layoff of 50%+ of Twitter's workforce (7,500 people) within 2 weeks generated: multiple WARN Act lawsuits (settled for $100M+), Glassdoor rating dropped from 3.9 to 2.9 (dramatically reducing future recruiting effectiveness), and significant survivor turnover (key employees chose to leave rather than work in the chaotic environment). The 'survivor syndrome' research: studies of post-layoff organizations consistently find: 35-50% of survivors say they are less productive after layoffs; 25-35% say they feel less committed to the organization. These productivity losses occur regardless of layoff speed; the severity correlates with perceived fairness of the process.

question: A company's HR team presents a 'people dashboard' to the board: Turnover rate 18%, Time-to-Fill 45 days, Engagement score 68/100, and Diversity representation 22% underrepresented groups in the workforce. The board asks: 'How do we know which of these to prioritize?' What framework should guide the prioritization?
options: All people metrics should always receive equal investment and priority

Business impact analysis, competitive benchmarks, and strategic goals determine prioritization: the prioritization framework: (1) Business impact analysis: which metric, if improved, would most directly affect business performance? If turnover is driving significant costs ($15M/year in replacement) and productivity gaps, it may have higher ROI than improving diversity representation by 2% in the short term; if the business strategy requires specific technical skills that are hard to find (time-to-fill problem), recruiting speed may have the highest immediate business impact; (2) Competitive benchmark: is 68/100 engagement above or below industry average? (Gallup reports 32% US employee engagement nationally, with significant variation by industry; 68% of a different scale may be excellent or poor depending on the benchmark); is 22% diversity below industry norm, legal compliance requirements, or customer representation? (3) Interdependencies: high turnover often has engagement as a root cause — improving engagement may simultaneously improve turnover; addressing both separately may be less efficient than addressing the root cause; (4) Strategic goals: if the board has a stated diversity commitment (public ESG report, investor expectations), the diversity metric has strategic prioritization beyond pure business impact; (5) Leading vs. lagging indicators: engagement (a leading indicator of future turnover) may be more valuable to improve than turnover (a lagging indicator that shows damage already done)
Diversity metrics should always be the top HR priority
Employee engagement is always more important than turnover metrics
explanation: Metrics prioritization in practice: the strategic context of each metric changes its priority. A company in a tight talent market (technology companies during 2020-2022) would prioritize time-to-fill and turnover above all else — talent availability constrained growth more than any other factor. A company with regulatory or ESG investor pressure might prioritize diversity metrics. A company with declining customer satisfaction might prioritize engagement (as the root cause of customer service quality). The causal chain: the ideal people metrics system traces the causal chain from people inputs to business outcomes: engagement → retention → productivity → customer satisfaction → revenue. Improving the root cause (engagement) produces improvements across all downstream metrics. The balanced scorecard approach to people metrics: Kaplan and Norton's original Balanced Scorecard included 'Learning and Growth' as one of four perspectives — recognizing that workforce capability and engagement are leading indicators of financial performance, not lagging ones. The board's question 'how do we know which to prioritize?' is the right question — it reflects an understanding that metrics are only valuable when they guide decisions, not when they are reported for reporting's sake.
